description: Failure cases of American retirees abroad. Anonymized and summarized from public news articles, court records, IRS data, and embassy advisories. No personally identifying information.

cases.summary: Dual citizen returned to US in 2011, learned of FBAR requirement only after returning. IRS originally assessed penalties per-account across 272 accounts spanning 5 years.
cases.lesson: FBAR penalties are per-form, not per-account (Bittner v. US, 2023). But even non-willful penalties of $10K per year compound fast — file before IRS contacts you.

cases.summary: Non-willful FBAR violation max penalty in 2026 (inflation-adjusted).
cases.lesson: Each year of non-filing stacks up to $16,536. Five years of unknown non-compliance = $82,680 even without willful intent.

cases.summary: Willful FBAR violation maximum penalty per form in 2026.
cases.amount_alternative: or 50% of account balance, whichever is greater
cases.lesson: Willful failure to file is treated as quasi-criminal. The 50% of balance rule can wipe out an entire retirement nest egg.

cases.summary: Failure to file Form 8938 (FATCA) on foreign assets over $50K threshold.
cases.additional_note: $50K added after IRS notification + 40% accuracy-related penalty on understated tax
cases.lesson: FATCA and FBAR are two separate filings on overlapping assets. Many retirees file one, miss the other.

cases.summary: Retiree dropped Medicare Part B while living abroad for 10 years. On return, lifetime late enrollment penalty of 10% per missed year applied.
cases.calculation: Base premium $185/mo x 12 x 100% surcharge = ~$2,200/yr extra. Over 20 years of remaining retirement = ~$44,000 in lifetime penalty cost on top of base premium.
cases.lesson: Even if you live abroad and never use Medicare, dropping Part B is permanent. The penalty applies forever, every month.

cases.summary: Retiree gone 63+ days without creditable drug coverage abroad triggers Part D lifetime penalty of 1% per month.
cases.amount_calculation: After 5 years abroad (60 months without creditable coverage), surcharge = 60% of national base premium ($36.78 in 2026) = ~$22/mo for life
cases.lesson: Foreign drug coverage usually does not count as 'creditable' under Medicare rules. Get a Medicare-recognized creditable coverage letter or expect a permanent surcharge.

cases.summary: US tourist/retiree needing air ambulance from Mexico to US.
cases.lesson: Medicare does not cover any care or evacuation abroad. Most US private insurance also excludes international evacuation. Standalone medevac membership ($300-500/yr) is the only viable hedge.

cases.summary: Hospitals in Mexico routinely require payment upfront and place credit card holds on admission for foreigners.
cases.lesson: Even insured retirees face out-of-pocket cash demand at admission. Plan for an emergency reserve separate from main savings, with high-limit credit card.

cases.summary: Many international health insurers cap new enrollments at age 70-75 or sharply raise rates after 65. Retirees who delay enrolling find themselves uninsurable.
cases.lesson: Lock in international insurance BEFORE you turn 65, ideally before relocating. Switching plans after a health event is nearly impossible.

cases.summary: American retiree submitted Spain Non-Lucrative Visa with $54,000/yr Social Security + pension income, but checking account balance was below the $2,600/month threshold. BLS Houston rejected.
cases.lesson: Spain NLV requires BOTH income AND a static savings cushion (~$30K). Many retirees pass the income test but fail the balance test.

cases.summary: Applicants with only a savings lump sum and no recurring monthly passive income receive automatic NLV rejection.
cases.lesson: Spain wants 12 months of consistent income proof. One large transfer 'looking like' savings does not qualify as passive income.

cases.summary: D7 Visa applicants rejected for lacking a 12-month rental agreement or owned property in Portugal at time of application.
cases.lesson: Portugal D7 requires real housing arranged BEFORE the visa is granted. Airbnb receipts and 'hotel for first month' plans get rejected.

cases.summary: Costa Rica Pensionado requires $1,000/mo from a single lifetime source. IRA distributions do not qualify. Several Americans rejected for using IRA income as proof.
cases.lesson: Income must be lifetime/guaranteed — Social Security and traditional pensions qualify, IRAs and 401(k)s do not.

cases.summary: Panama Pensionado applicants rejected because pension letter did not explicitly state 'for life' / 'lifetime' designation.
cases.lesson: Get a re-issued pension letter using the exact phrase 'payable for the life of the beneficiary' before applying.

cases.summary: Applicants using investment/financial-instrument income (dividends, brokerage payouts) for Pensionado are denied — only true pensions qualify.
cases.lesson: Pensionado is for pension recipients. If your retirement income is brokerage-based, use a Rentista or Friendly Nations route instead.

cases.summary: Chase, Bank of America, and Wells Fargo close US accounts upon discovery of foreign residential address under USA PATRIOT Act CIP rules.
cases.lesson: Notify your bank BEFORE moving and ask in writing whether they permit foreign addresses. Move to USAA, Schwab, or a CMRA + state without income tax before leaving.

cases.summary: Vanguard, Fidelity, Morgan Stanley, Merrill Lynch, Edward Jones, Ameriprise, TIAA, USAA all restricted or terminated expat brokerage accounts since 2024. Forced liquidation triggers immediate capital gains tax.
cases.amount_capital_gains_impact_usd_estimate: 10-37% of unrealized gains depending on holding period
cases.lesson: Move positions IN-KIND via ACATS transfer to Interactive Brokers or Schwab International. Never let the firm force a cash liquidation.

cases.summary: Charles Schwab notified US citizens in 5 specific countries that accounts will be frozen — liquidation only, no new investment.
cases.lesson: Even 'expat-friendly' brokers carry country risk. Hold at minimum two custodians in two jurisdictions for portfolio resilience.

cases.summary: Retirees abroad surprised that up to 85% of Social Security remains US-taxable. FEIE does not apply to Social Security income.
cases.lesson: Foreign Tax Credit (not FEIE) is the only mechanism to avoid double tax on Social Security. Check whether your country has a treaty that exempts SS specifically (e.g., Canada, UK, Germany do; Mexico does not).

cases.summary: American retiree bought a 'foreign mutual fund' or non-US ETF (e.g., Irish-domiciled UCITS) and triggered PFIC rules. Default 'excess distribution' regime taxes at top ordinary rate plus interest charge.
cases.lesson: Never own non-US-domiciled mutual funds or ETFs as a US person. Even one European ETF in a foreign brokerage account triggers Form 8621 and PFIC treatment.

cases.summary: Retiree with $2M+ net worth renouncing US citizenship triggered exit tax — deemed sale of all assets at fair market value on expatriation date.
cases.lesson: Plan renunciation BEFORE assets cross $2M, or work with a tax pro to structure timing. Once 'covered expatriate', tax can hit retirement accounts at full ordinary rates.

cases.summary: Retiree who did not file US returns abroad for 5 years faced standard failure-to-file (5%/mo capped 25%) + failure-to-pay (0.5%/mo) + FBAR + FATCA simultaneously.
cases.lesson: Penalties stack independently. A $20K unpaid tax becomes ~$30K with penalties+interest within a few years. Use IRS Streamlined Filing Compliance to wipe penalties.

cases.summary: Cigna Global/IMG/GeoBlue premiums for retiree couples often double between ages 65 and 75 due to age-band repricing.
cases.lesson: Model your insurance cost as a curve, not a flat line. Build a separate health-cost reserve growing 5-7%/yr to absorb age-banded jumps.

cases.summary: Retirees using a Commercial Mail Receiving Agent (CMRA) address (private mailbox) to keep US banking — many banks now flag known CMRA street addresses and force closure or freeze.
cases.lesson: Verify the CMRA address is not on the USPS public CMRA list before relying on it for bank KYC. Newer 'virtual mailbox' services may be flagged.

cases.summary: D7 applicants rejected for 'invalid travel insurance' — short-term travel policies that exclude pre-existing conditions or do not meet Schengen minimum coverage (€30K).
cases.lesson: Buy a Schengen-compliant health insurance policy with at least 12 months validity and confirmed pre-existing condition coverage BEFORE the consulate appointment.
